speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I'm interviewing Paul Oppenheimer in Solihull, England. Paul, you were talking just then about the deportations. What memories do you have of people being deported?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the main memory that I have is at school, really. We were now exclusively Jewish schools and Jewish teachers. And it was noticeable that every week, sometimes every day, when we got to school in the morning, there would be people missing-- students missing and even teachers missing. And after major raids, major deportations kind of thing, all the classes would have to be rearranged. And this was obviously a very sad occasion, every morning, to see that some of your friends had disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your understanding of what was going on at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the word was, basically, that people were being sent for resettlement in the East, was the terminology that they used. We didn't really know about concentration camps, or if we did know about concentration camps-- I mean, our parents probably did know because there were concentration camps even before the war. Dachau and all that were set up in 1933, so, yes, we knew that there were camps. And this was the intention-- to send us to the East. But we had no idea of what was really happening in the camps. And we had no idea of names like Auschwitz and all that sort of thing at that stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just another part of the game. The whole thing was almost like a game, really. That every month there would be yet another restriction, another rule about you couldn't do this, you couldn't do that, and you had to do that and do this. So we took it in the way-- we'd had to move from [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE], from [INAUDIBLE] to Amsterdam. We had to go to a Jewish school. We weren't allowed in the shops. We weren't allowed to play sports, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now we were going to be sent to camps. Obviously we could see that each step was a step backwards-- deteriorating kind of thing. But nobody really resisted. Everybody tried to get out of it or not be on the list or not be deported as much as they could, but there was never any joint action in any kind of way against all the rules because they were introduced in such a step by step fashion. Everybody always said, well, we've lived with all this. Just this extra bit that we have to be at home at night between 8:00 and 6:00 isn't really [INAUDIBLE] We usually are anyway kind of thing, so we'll put up with it. And then next month, we're not allowed to shop except between 3:00 and 5:00, and we say, well, it's bad, but we can probably put up with it. And in that way, the screw was turned all the time, and we put up with it. If in one go they'd started off by doing everything at once, there probably would have been a big resistance, not only by the Jews but the Dutch would probably have helped us kind of thing, and something would be done. But in all these ways, the Germans were extremely clever and efficient and methodical. And we succumbed, and we just did what they asked us to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So more and more people were deported from Amsterdam. We noticed it at the schools. Eventually, for example, the whole old age home, the [INAUDIBLE]-- one day, everybody who was in the [INAUDIBLE] was deported to camp-- this [? transit ?] camp called Westerbork in the eastern part of Holland. We'd heard about that everybody was going there. We didn't know what was happening there and where people went from there. So they went in early 1943. The whole old age home was taken away and deported, including our four grandparents, to Westerbork. Can I just mention the sports again, [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE]? It was a highlight. [INAUDIBLE] And on of the few things that I do remember is that in Amsterdam, we were not allowed to play most sports, and not with other people. Only with Jews we were allowed to play sports. And this was for the first time when I played table tennis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, actually, I became a bit of a table tennis star in the Midlands kind of thing. But it started in Amsterdam, where I played table tennis for the first time. It was very strange because there was no rubber for the bats. We played with all kind of funny concoctions. I remember having linoleum on the batting surface for playing table tennis. And another thing that we played there, very surprisingly, was hockey. Hockey was always a big game in Holland. And we played mixed hockey because there weren't enough boys or girls to play individual teams. So we played mixed hockey in those days. So those are some of the things I remember. Useless things for these purposes, but that's-- mine seems to have been-- I can remember car number plates from many years ago, and sports I can remember, but don't remember the important things like Judaism, I'm sorry to say. Another thing that, perhaps, mentioning Judaism is that I should have had my bar mitzvah, actually, at the time when we were in in [INAUDIBLE], but I didn't have it. I would imagine that there was no possibility at that time of proper synagogue services, although I may be wrong. But one way or another, neither me nor [INAUDIBLE] two years later had their bar mitzvah in Holland. We had it afterwards, after the war, in England. So whether we were aware of it at that time, I'm not quite sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else should I say about Amsterdam? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Basically, things just got worse, and more and more people were deported, and eventually, it was our turn. In 1943, there were very big raids, and in our case, it was the 20th of June, 1943. There were two very big raids-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] as they call them. I'm not sure if that's a Dutch word or a German word or an English word-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was basically roundups. They cordoned off the whole of the south Amsterdam area where all the Jews, especially all the German refugee Jews, lived. It was cordoned off by military vehicles and tanks and all this sort of thing, and the German army and Dutch army and police, et cetera, very early in the morning, because before the curfew finished on  the Sunday morning, before 6 o'clock in the morning, when Jews were not allowed out. So all Jews had to be in their home or were supposed to be in their home. And so they cordoned it, off and nobody could get in or out. They wouldn't let Dutch people in or out either. Everything was just shut off completely. And the SS or Gestapo or whoever it was came around to all the doors. There were loudspeakers going around saying all Jews come out with their luggage for resettlement, deportation. And individual officers came around to all our addresses because the [INAUDIBLE] had a list of all Jews and the addresses where all the Jews lived in Amsterdam. This was part of the earlier registration process, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew where we lived. And they came around to our door and banged on the door and told us we had half an hour to come out into the nearby square. [INAUDIBLE] thinks that the SS actually missed us, and that they couldn't find us, and that there was a Nazi sympathizer on the other side of the street who pointed out to them that they'd missed us and they came back for us. I don't know whether it's true, but they came in any case.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should, perhaps, say that in Holland, there was a Nazi party who collaborated with the Germans, with the Nazis, but it was a relatively small number of people called NSB. That was the Dutch Nazi party. But they were severely dealt with after the war by the Dutch people. The vast majority of Dutch people were not on the side of the Nazis, but there were some. And there were, of course, benefits of being on the Nazi side during the war, during the occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry-- do you remember them actually coming to your door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I mean, I suppose they must have banged on the door. I don't suppose I opened the door. It would've been my father who would open the door. And they would tell us we had to come out. We could hear it on the loud speakers, and we could see all the commotion outside, with people walking up and down with all their luggage.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help pack your own suitcase?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were already packed. I mean, this was another law that I haven't mentioned because there's millions of laws. There was a law where it said that all Jews had to be prepared to go to a camp. And we had to be ready with a suitcase or a rucksack. And it tells you in my book and  all of [INAUDIBLE] you can see what you had to prepare. You had to have trousers, shirts, pullover, and warm clothes and shoes, and a mug and a fork and knife and spoon. And it tells you all the things that you had to bring with you. And you had to be prepared at all times for this eventuality. It was more the food that was the difficult thing. All these other clothes kind of things we had ready for months, because these deportations by that time-- June '43, this was-- it had been going on for a year, where people had been either picked up in the street or volunteered, taken away according to lists or caught like this in roundups, cordoned-off areas. But that day was a very big one. And I believe that some 10,000 people were deported that day from Amsterdam. It was a very big [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- one of the last. Because my father, of course, immediately showed him his exemption from the [INAUDIBLE], but they said the exemptions were no longer valid kind of thing, and we had to go. And he then tried the British nationality, and those people that came to the door apparently couldn't deal with that. And they said, you'd have to deal with that later on. And I know I can remember seeing my father arguing later on with higher officials, kind of thing, obviously about the British nationality of Eve, and all that sort of thing. But it didn't seem to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, like everybody else, we were all collected up in a square, the top of the road, top of our road there, and then we're put in trucks that were taken to a nearby station, [INAUDIBLE] railway station. There, we were put on the train of cattle trucks. And in the trucks, we were taken to this camp, Westerbork-- [INAUDIBLE] Westerbork as it's called-- which was a transit camp. What do you remember of the journey in the truck from Amsterdam to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first bit was in a truck, and then in the cattle trucks kind of thing, yeah. Nothing, really. I suppose [INAUDIBLE]. It was not a big journey. Probably take a couple of hours, something like that. I think it was a lot of waiting around and all that because the [INAUDIBLE] started, as I said, very early in the morning. And it was probably late in the evening by the time we got to Westerbork and sorted out and everything else. So we spent all day hanging around. But it was all fairly orderly and methodical kind of thing. And I didn't really see too much in the way of beating or shouting or any of that. Everybody just did as they were told. Everybody knew about it because we'd seen, heard, about these roundups before. So we realized that eventually it probably would be our turn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was with you on that journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember any of the names. It was just father, mother, Eve, [INAUDIBLE] and me. But there would be other people there that we knew, but we just can't remember [INAUDIBLE]. I've found lots of letters and lots of names of people, but I can't really recall them. I can't remember them in any kind of way. I wouldn't recognize them if I saw them again, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was basically the end of Amsterdam. [INAUDIBLE] grandparents had come before. And when we got to Westerbork, we were registered again. They had registration cards there, and that took quite a long time to get all that sorted out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, we were taken to assigned barracks. And there were separate barracks for men and women. And I went into a barracks with my father. I can't off-hand remember the number, but we have got it in all our documentation. And [INAUDIBLE], Eve, and my mother went to another barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was being registered like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just stood in a queue in front of tables where all our details were written down. And we had some sort of identity cards. I don't have copies of those. I can't remember, really, what they looked like. But perhaps later or perhaps now, a very important feature was that-- in our case, on our registration cards in Westerbork, we had a blue stamp. And it turned out that this blue stamp meant that you were destined for exchange purposes. You were held back for exchange purposes. This blue stamp, we soon found out, really, by comparing our position, our situation in Westerbork, with lots of other people, and who had blue stamps and who didn't. So we soon found out what it was for. It was for exchange purposes. And might as well say now that the people who had blue stamps in Westerbork were people who had allied connections, such as one member of the family that was British or American. And then there were people who had what they called certificates for Palestine. Basically, they were entry visas to go to Palestine, as it was called in those days. And many Jewish people in Holland had been able to get these Palestine certificates. Obviously, people in Holland realized the benefits of these exchanges. Everybody must have known about this exchange business so everybody desperately tried to get these Palestine certificates. And, again, we have notes here that we applied for Palestine certificates from our aunt who was living in Palestine at that time, Liesel. We applied for certificates for our paternal grandparents, which were her parents, but we never got them in time. And we also applied for Palestine certificates for us, for our family, in case the British thing didn't work. But we don't think we ever got those, in the end. There was a lot of correspondence that they'd been applied for and all this sort of thing. But we gather the only time they worked is if you actually had the number on the certificate. And all this went by the Red Cross and by the Swiss embassy and by this, that, and everything else. It took ages to get these things sorted out. And, in our case, we don't think we ever actually got the Palestine certificates. But a lot of other people did, and, as I say, these people, [INAUDIBLE] that lived across the road from us in [INAUDIBLE], they had the Palestine certificates, and they were actually exchanged. So it did work. The other people who had the blue stamps were people who had entry visas for various South American countries like Nicaragua and Colombia and Venezuela and all that sort of thing. We understand that you could buy those visas in Holland before you went [INAUDIBLE]. You couldn't buy them in Westerbork anymore. But in Amsterdam and previously, you could buy those certificates for loads of money. And in Holland, there were lots of very rich Jews, Dutch Jews-- not the German refugees, but the Dutch Jews. There were many who were in the diamond industry and they had loads of money. And there were many who were in stores and shops. Some of the very biggest stores in Holland were owned by Jews, and they had lots of money, and they could afford to buy these visas. It was interesting that the interviewer who interviewed me on this other program for this [INAUDIBLE] video, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], he, at that point in the interview, said his family also had a lot of those visas for Ecuador and all that sort of thing. So he must have been rich. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So those people who had these visas-- they also had the blue stamp. Now, some people sort of are very critical-- you know, why did the Germans allow all this kind of thing? Basically, they wanted to get all their Germans back, and they were quite happy to give away Jews in order to get Germans back to their country. And that was basically the plan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they could see that normally, no other country was going to take Jews just like that. I mean, an interesting thing was that there had been this conference in [? Evian ?] in 1938 where all the countries of the world assembled in [? Evian ?] on Lake Geneva to decide what to do about the Jewish problem in Germany and on the continent, and how they were going to help the refugees. And the outcome of it all was they all said, yes, we ought to help, but no single country offered take any of the refugees in. And basically, that was the situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans were well aware that nobody was going to take the Jews, and nobody was going to help the Jews unless it was on an exchange basis. And there was some reason why the British people would take these Jews. And the only reason why the Brits would take Jews would be because they were British.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the same way, Ecuador and Venezuela-- if there were Jews who had official visas to enter Venezuela and Ecuador, then these countries might take those Jews. Many people say that these visas were actually forged and all that, and that they would never have got in anyway. And I don't think any of them were ever exchanged, but that was the idea. And so all those people had blue stamps in Westerbork. And we were eventually moved around from the barracks that we were in originally in Westerbork to other barracks, where all these people with blue stamps seem to have been congregated. Because we were held back in Westerbork, like in Amsterdam where we were not rounded up for a long time because my father worked for the [INAUDIBLE], and maybe also because of our British connection/ the same happened again in Westerbork. We stayed in Westerbork from the [? 20th ?] of June, 1943 until the [? 1st ?] of February, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in Westerbork for a very, very long time because, basically, it was a transit camp, and most people didn't stay in Westerbork more than a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was lots of good things about Westerbork, but the bad thing about Westerbork was that every Monday afternoon, there would be a train of cattle trucks-- of about 20 cattle trucks. Would roll into the middle of Westerbork camp. The railway track went through the middle of the camp. These 20 cattle trucks would appear every Monday afternoon. And on Monday evening, there would be published a list of approximately 1,000 Jews. And they had to report Tuesday morning to go into the cattle trucks for resettlement in the East.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean, in Westerbork, we had again the same thing. There was a [INAUDIBLE], a Jewish council, that was set up to administer all these things. And this is really where there was more trouble, because Westerbork was a camp that was built before the war by the Dutch government, not by the Germans and the Nazis. It was before the war, built by the Dutch government for Jewish refugees from Germany. We came early, in 1936, and we could make our way. But later, people who came from Germany and Austria and all that who got out without money, without furniture, without anything kind of thing-- they didn't have anything, and they were all put into Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were Jewish refugees from Germany, mainly, who lived in Westerbork before the war, before the Germans invaded. When the Germans invaded and took over, they took over this camp, Westerbork, and decided that this was a good place for a transit camp. And those Jews who were already there, the Jewish refugees, German refugees-- they already lived there and they stayed there. So they had the best places. There were houses. There were houses in Westerbork, and these German refugees lived in houses, whereas later arrivals all had to live in barracks. And they had all sorts of preferential treatments. And, of course, when they started a Jewish council, it was the German Jews, the refugee Jews, that were all on this German counsel because they were there first.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, I mean, this is one of the things we've learned from the camps. If you're going to go to camp, you want to be there first. And they had all the best jobs, and the thing. And this, of course, later then caused a lot of friction because these were all the Germans who were refugees, really. Were not even Dutch Jews. And they were compiling the list of who was going to be deported on Monday evening. And, naturally, their friends weren't on the list. It was other people. There was no doubt a lot of bribery in all sorts of ways because nobody really wanted to go on these transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it-- the trouble-- manifest itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know, really. But when you talk to various survivors, you can get the gist of it, kind of thing. [? Leon ?] [INAUDIBLE], for example-- don't now whether you know him kind of thing. Auschwitz survivor. He's particularly bitter, but then he had a very interesting special case, a bit similar to ours. He's one of the few other British survivors of the holocaust, [INAUDIBLE]. And he lived in [? Rotterdam ?] with his wife and child, British-born. He gave his passport, British passport, to Dutch friends for safekeeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was picked up, sent to Westerbork. And in Westerbork, he said he was British. And they said, where are your papers? So he said, I'll get them. And he went back to Rotterdam, and he found that these Dutch people had burned his passport because they didn't want to be caught with the British passport of a Jew. So in Westerbork-- he was in Westerbork saying he was British, but he couldn't prove it. So he had to go via this chain, the Swiss embassy and the Hague, to Switzerland and then to Britain, to get a copy of his passport or his birth certificate. Did you meet him in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was there before us, [? long ?] before us-- early in '42. But I've met him since and I've heard his story.

speaker: interviewer
text: But with your own experiences, what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our case, we were kept in Westerbork for a very long time. And I've told you now the bad part about Westerbork, which is the deportations every Tuesday morning of around about 1,000 Jews. But, for us, we just stayed in Westerbork all the time because, as we found out, we had the blue stamp, and they hadn't decided yet what they were going to do with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your grandparents? Because they didn't have protection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. When we got to Westerbork, two grandparents had already been deported. And the other grandparents, the paternal grandparents, were still in Westerbork. But within a couple of weeks of us arriving there-- I mean, we saw them every day because Westerbork, basically, was quite a good camp, really, where you could meet people and all those sort of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we met them every day. We saw them every day. But very soon after we arrived, one night they were on the list kind of thing

speaker: interviewee
text: And every evening, Monday evening, in each barrack, the names of the people who had to be transported were read out of those who were in that barrack. Only those in that barrack were read out. And it was, of course, always in alphabetical order, so it was a great excitement kind of thing when they went through in alphabetical order and they got near to your letter kind of thing and then they passed your letter and you were not on the list. [? This ?] [? was ?] obviously a great feeling of relief, relaxation kind of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't actually hear them being called out, but we found pretty soon, because they were not in our barracks. We found out that they were due for deportation next morning. My father tried, with his German connections and this [INAUDIBLE] connections and his British connections and all these sort of things, to prevent them being deported, but it didn't help. So next morning, they were deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all had to stay in our barracks in the morning, so we didn't see them [INAUDIBLE] leave. Nobody was allowed out at morning on Tuesday mornings, except the people that were being deported. Until the train had left, nobody was outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they were deported to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after the war. We didn't know anything then. They were for resettlement in the East. But after the war, at the [INAUDIBLE], we found all the records because the Germans had been extremely meticulous-- both the Germans and the Dutch-- and both of them have records of everybody who went through Westerbork-- their date of birth, where they went, and when they died. And we found out that all four grandparents went to Sobibor, and all four grandparents were killed the day after they arrived. In fact, from Westerbork, 34,000 people went to Sobibor. 19 survived. It was far worse than Auschwitz. In Auschwitz, 58,000 people went to Auschwitz from Westerbork and 800 survived. At Sobibor, nobody survived. We've seen models of Sobibor and Treblinka. Basically, you arrived and you went to the gas chamber and you went to the crematorium, and that was it. And it was a very small camp. It was just a pure killing camp. It was not like Auschwitz. Big, and lots of barracks, nobody stayed. They had a small team of people who helped with the gassing and the bodies and all that. And that was it. There was hardly and barracks in Treblinka and Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you found out about that after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Found out about that after the war. So that was the four grandparents, yes. We didn't know that in Westerbork, just that they had all been deported to the East for resettlement. I think perhaps I'll stop at that point.
